stimulus_text: Jun passed the clockmaker's shop every afternoon, but he had never seen the window empty. Usually it glittered with brass faces and tiny swinging pendulums. On Friday, only one clock remained: a plain wooden one with a stopped second hand. A note beside it read, 'Back when time is ready.' Jun smiled at the joke, then noticed the shop door was unlocked. Inside, the air smelt of dust and lemon oil. On the counter lay a parcel addressed to his neighbour, Mrs Vale, who had missed school pickup all week. Jun could have left it there. Instead, he tucked it under his arm and hurried down the lane, suddenly aware that the silent clock was not the strangest part of the afternoon.

stimulus_text: Mangroves grow where land and sea meet. Their roots look tangled, but the tangle is useful. Small fish hide among the roots until they are large enough to swim in open water. Crabs loosen the mud as they feed, allowing air to reach buried roots. Mangroves also trap floating leaves and sand, gradually building a firmer shoreline. When storms push waves inland, mangrove forests slow the water and reduce erosion. Some people once cleared mangroves because the muddy edges seemed empty. Scientists now describe them as nurseries, filters and natural sea walls. Protecting a mangrove area usually means protecting many other species that depend on it.
